semantic.description: Let \( f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R} \) be a continuous function. The curvilinear trapezoid associated with \( f \) on \( [a,b] \) is the planar region bounded by the graph of \( f \), the x-axis, and the vertical lines \( x=a \) and \( x=b \).

Let \( f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R} \) be a bounded function. If the lower sums and upper sums associated with \( f \) converge to the same limit as the norm of the partition tends to zero, then this common value is called the definite integral of \( f \) over \( [a,b] \), denoted by \( \int_a^b f(x)\,dx \).

semantic.statement: Let \( f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R} \) be continuous and let \( F \) be an antiderivative of \( f \) on \( [a,b] \). Then \( \int_a^b f(x)\,dx = F(b)-F(a) \).
Let \( f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R} \) be continuous and define \( F(x)=\int_a^x f(t)\,dt \). Then \( F \) is differentiable on \( (a,b) \) and satisfies \( F'(x)=f(x) \).
If \( f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R} \) is continuous, then there exists \( c\in(a,b) \) such that \( \int_a^b f(x)\,dx = f(c)(b-a) \).

semantic.description: The average value of an integrable function \( f \) on \( [a,b] \) is defined by \( f_{avg}=\frac{1}{b-a}\int_a^b f(x)\,dx \).
The definite integral \( \int_a^b f(x)\,dx \) represents a signed area in which portions of the graph above the x-axis contribute positively and portions below the x-axis contribute negatively.
The geometric area between the graph of \( f \) and the x-axis over \( [a,b] \) is given by \( \int_a^b |f(x)|\,dx \).
An improper integral is an integral in which either the interval of integration is unbounded or the integrand becomes unbounded within the interval.
An improper integral is said to converge if the limit defining it exists and is finite.
An improper integral is said to diverge if the limit defining it does not exist or is infinite.
